posts.author_name: Bridge Counsel’s Fractional GC Article | Bridge Counsels
posts.content:
With the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act now in place, organisations in India are being pushed to rethink how they collect, store, and use personal data.
But compliance isn’t just about policies or paperwork. It requires real alignment between legal, tech, and business teams.

Many companies struggle not because the law is unclear, but because execution is. Gaps in processes, awareness, and internal coordination often create the biggest risks.

This is where the role of a Fractional General Counsel becomes strategic; helping translate legal requirements into practical systems, embedding compliance into day-to-day operations, and ensuring organisations stay ahead of evolving regulations.

The bigger shift?
Data protection is moving from a compliance function to a governance and trust-building tool.

Swipe through for a quick breakdown of our article on what it takes to build a DPDP-ready organisation.

Article Link: https://lnkd.in/gZy2TnqH

Authored by: Gaurav Gupta and RajNanda 

#DPDP #PrivacyLaw #BridgeCounsels #FractionalGC #Startups
posts.summary: With the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act now in place, organisations in India are being pushed to rethink how they collect, store, and use personal data. But compliance isn’t just about policies or paperwork. It requires real alignment between legal, tech, and business teams.

posts.author_name: #sebiindia #aifindia #privateequity #indianvc #startupfunding #sebi #startupindia | Achintya Goyal
posts.content:
The legal structure that blocks most Indian startups from accessing institutional capital is not the market.

It is the paperwork.

India's private capital ecosystem operates through SEBI-registered Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs).

These are the vehicles through which domestic VCs, PEs, and angel networks deploy capital into Indian startups.

And understanding how they work  legally is not optional for founders who want to raise serious money.



WHAT IS AN AIF?

An Alternative Investment Fund under the SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 is any fund established in India which collects funds from sophisticated investors — whether Indian or foreign — for investing in accordance with a defined investment policy.



There are 3 categories:

CATEGORY I AIF: Invests in start-ups, SMEs, social ventures, infrastructure. Eligible for certain tax pass-through benefits. Includes Angel Funds (minimum corpus ₹10 crore, maximum investment per company ₹25 crore via the angel fund route).

CATEGORY II AIF: PE funds, debt funds, fund of funds — do not undertake leverage except for day-to-day operations. Most India-based VC funds operate as Category II AIFs.

CATEGORY III AIF: Hedge funds, funds that use complex or diverse trading strategies.



WHY THIS MATTERS FOR FOUNDERS:
The AIF structure determines:

→ How quickly a fund can deploy into you (internal investment committee processes, SEBI-mandated diversification limits)

→ What documentation you need to provide (the investment agreement must comply with AIF regulations)

→ How exits are structured (lock-in periods, distribution waterfalls)
→ Whether the fund can invest in your sector (sector restrictions apply to Category I AIFs)

And there is a provision most founders never ask about:

Under SEBI AIF Regulations — an AIF cannot invest more than 25% of its investable funds in a single investee company.

This means: a ₹100 crore fund can invest a maximum of ₹25 crore in your startup in a single round.

If your round is larger — you need multiple AIFs, or the fund needs to route through a co-investment vehicle.

The Indian startup founders who understand how domestic institutional capital is structured, are the ones who raise it faster, on better terms, with fewer surprises.


#SEBIIndia #AIFIndia #PrivateEquity #IndianVC #StartupFunding #SEBI #StartupIndia
posts.summary: The legal structure that blocks most Indian startups from accessing institutional capital is not the market. It is the paperwork. India's private capital ecosystem operates through SEBI-registered Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs).

posts.author_name:
India Is In The Vegetative Phase(Coma) of DPDPA in April,2026
I call it "The Vegetative Compliance Syndrome", a condition in which an organisation is legally active, commercially vital, and privacy… | Anandaday Misshra | 10 comments
posts.content:
India Is In The Vegetative Phase(Coma) of DPDPA in April,2026
I call it "The Vegetative Compliance Syndrome", a condition in which an organisation is legally active, commercially vital, and privacy dead.
Data flows. Consent is collected. Notices are pushed. Every metric of operation looks alive.But, the compliance brain stem is offline and perhaps last till March,2027 and for adventurous organisations it can be any day.

Why I say this?
The boardrooms are full of three things. Belief. Illusion. Own Versions.
1.Belief — "We will manage. The deadline will slip. Nothing happens in India on time."
2.Illusion — "IT has handled it. The privacy policy is updated. We are compliant."
3.Own Versions — "Our interpretation is that existing terms cover consent. Our counsel agrees."
Three symptoms. One diagnosis. Vegetative.Hence, it is "The Vegetative Compliance Syndrome".

In GDPR, on the very first day two breaches were reported( We are releasing a white paper shortly on this issue)

So the next question arises!  
When does India come out of the coma?"
Not on 13 May 2027. That is only the day the alarm rings.
The real awakening begins the day the first ₹250 crore order lands on a Board table, a press release hits the front page, and a CEO discovers his counsel told him what he wanted to hear instead of what the statute said.

That day may arrive between June 2027 and December 2027. Sooner, if the Board picks an early example. The first order is always the loudest.

Until then, India breathes through templates. Blinks through cookie banners. Moves its fingers through privacy policies that read like translations of GDPR from 2018.

Pick your coma exit. The clock is no longer an idea. It is on the Gazette.

Deepti Bhatia DS Mahajani Jai Shankar Sharma Rohit Lalwani Mrinal Bharat Ram Mridusha G. Rakesh Maheshwari Athul Gaikwad | 10 comments on LinkedIn
posts.summary:
India Is In The Vegetative Phase(Coma) of DPDPA in April,2026
I call it "The Vegetative Compliance Syndrome", a condition in which an organisation is legally active, commercially vital, and privacy dead. Data flows. Consent is collected.

posts.author_name:
The most successful funds in India may be too big to beat the market.

That's not a manager problem. It's a size problem.

Here's the math 👇
For a ₹1 lakh crore fund
posts.content:
The most successful funds in India may be too big to beat the market.

That's not a manager problem. It's a size problem.

Here's the math 👇
For a ₹1 lakh crore fund: 
▪ 0.5% position in a small-cap = ₹500 crore 
▪ Stock goes 4x → gain of ₹1,500 crore 
▪ NAV impact: ~1.5%

The manager was right. The investor barely noticed.

Getting the idea right is not enough. Position sizing determines whether it matters.

This is the structural trap of scale — and why the PMS vs MF debate shouldn't start with tax.

Three parts:
1/ AUM size and investable market cap tend to converge (see image). At scale, funds are pushed toward larger, more efficiently priced companies — where persistent mispricing is harder to find.

2/ In a large fund house, the manager is an employee. They leave. In a boutique PMS, the decision-maker is usually the founder. Incentives are different — not perfectly, but materially.

3/ Tax drag in PMS is real — but secondary. The only justification for it is sustained post-fee outperformance.

I exited my MF portfolio in March 2025 — funds that had delivered well over a long period. The managers were good. The constraint was structural.

Full piece on Substack 👇 
Link in comments.

Nextmil | ARN-320332 | APRN-01303 | AMFI Registered MFD & APMI Registered PMS Distributor
posts.summary: The most successful funds in India may be too big to beat the market. That's not a manager problem. It's a size problem.

posts.author_name: #startupindia #fundingnews #fintech #primeinvestor #investment #rainmatte #zerodha #portfoliomanagement #wealthmanagement | Startup Crunch
posts.content:
Chennai-based wealthtech platform PrimeInvestor has raised Rs 19.5 crore in seed funding from Rainmatter by Zerodha, the investment arm of Zerodha, in what marks the startup's first institutional funding round. The capital will be used to scale PrimeInvestor's recently launched portfolio management services and broaden its product lineup as the company transitions from a research and advisory platform to a full-stack wealth management provider.

Founded in 2019 by fintech veterans Srikanth Meenakshi, Vidya Bala, and Bhavana Acharya, PrimeInvestor built its reputation on independent mutual fund research and recommendations before launching its PMS offering in mid-January. The service uses a multi-asset approach spanning equities, debt, mutual funds, ETFs, and gold across three strategies — Balanced, Growth, and Ultra — with a minimum investment of Rs 50 lakh per PAN, as mandated by SEBI.

#StartupIndia #FundingNews #Fintech #PrimeInvestor #Investment #Rainmatte #Zerodha #PortfolioManagement #WealthManagement

posts.content:
Glad to have Deepesh Gosavi in the invite-only forum for Top 1% Leaders - India.

At Canara Robeco Asset Management Co. Ltd., technology is not just an enabling function.

It is the operating backbone behind trust, continuity, control, and scale.

Because in asset management,
technology is never only about infrastructure.

It is about ensuring reliability.
Protecting processes.
Driving efficiency.
And helping the institution move with confidence.

Raw systems can show:

what is running
what is delayed
what is secure

They do not show:

what the business needs next
what must be modernized first
what kind of operating discipline creates long-term resilience

That clarity comes from leadership.

Leaders like Deepesh Gosavi understand that technology leadership is built at the intersection of process, execution, governance, cloud transformation, and security.

They think in:

technology strategy
process efficiency
cloud and migration
budget discipline
operational resilience

And that is what makes this recognition meaningful.

Deepesh’s journey reflects steady, long-horizon leadership.

From his early years across infrastructure and systems management to his long-standing rise within Canara Robeco Mutual Fund, and now as Chief Technology Officer at Canararobeco Asset Management Co. Limited, his path reflects consistency, trust, and deep institutional understanding.

Having led roles across IT operations, office infrastructure, project management, analytics, budgeting, and security, he represents the kind of leader who does not just manage technology.

He strengthens the institution through it. | 10 comments on LinkedIn

posts.author_name: #fiduciaries #luxembourg #regulatedfinance #comodigitallife | Angela N. | 11 comments
posts.content:
I keep having the same conversation with fiduciaries.

Respected office. Strong compliance record. Long client relationships. Families, holding structures, cross-border vehicles.

And behind it all: a payments and banking stack held together by six institutions, four onboarding flows, two reconciliation spreadsheets, and one exhausted back-office team.

This is not an edge case. It is the rule.

Most fiduciaries operate with fragmented banking infrastructure because no single traditional institution was designed for the structures they serve. Data lives across multiple portals. Liquidity is unclear until Friday. Reconciliation becomes a weekly ritual. Regulatory reporting depends on systems that were never meant to work together.

So the fiduciary’s real value — judgment, discretion, long-term trust — gets diverted into spreadsheet work instead of client work.

That is an access problem.

The treasury infrastructure large corporates take for granted — real-time visibility, multi-entity account architecture, consolidated compliance — has historically been out of reach for fiduciaries. Not because they were unqualified, but because traditional banking infrastructure was built for a different kind of client.

COMO was built for that gap: a regulated, CSSF-supervised payment infrastructure where accounts, transactions, and compliance checks live in one architecture.

Not stitched together from vendors. One regulated environment.

The fiduciaries I spoke with did not want a new bank. They wanted to stop apologizing to their clients for how long reconciliation takes.

That is not a payment story. That is a trust story.
 
#Fiduciaries #Luxembourg #RegulatedFinance #COMODigitalLife
 | 11 comments on LinkedIn
posts.summary: I keep having the same conversation with fiduciaries. Respected office. Strong compliance record.

posts.author_name:
A digital banking platform came to us with a scaling issue.

But nothing was actually broken.

Until volume increased.

Then everything slowed down.

We dug in.
- It wasn’t infrastructure.
- It… | Hamza Shahzad | 18 comments
posts.content:
A digital banking platform came to us with a scaling issue.

But nothing was actually broken.

Until volume increased.

Then everything slowed down.

We dug in.
- It wasn’t infrastructure.
- It wasn’t features.

It was decisions.
- fraud checks still partly manual.
- compliance spread across systems.
- support re-checking data the system already knew.

So we didn’t add more tools.

We changed the decision layer.

We introduced Agentic AI inside the workflow not as a feature, but as execution logic.

Now:
- transactions self-route based on risk.
- compliance triggers instantly.
- routine requests resolve without human touch.

Same platform. Same stack.

Just fewer humans stuck in decisions the system should own.

That’s when it became obvious:

Digital banking doesn’t break on features. It breaks on decisions. | 18 comments on LinkedIn
posts.summary: A digital banking platform came to us with a scaling issue. But nothing was actually broken. Until volume increased.

posts.author_name: Is Your AI Audit-Ready? The New Requirements for Finance and GRC | SAPinsider
posts.content:
Is Your AI Audit Ready?
AI is moving quickly into SAP finance and GRC workflows, from financial close and invoice lifecycle management to access governance, cybersecurity, and continuous controls.
But for senior leaders, the real question is no longer simply whether AI can automate the work. It is whether AI-supported decisions are explainable, traceable, governed, and audit-ready.
In our new SAPinsider blog, “Is Your AI Audit-Ready? The New Requirements for Finance and GRC,” we look at what audit-ready AI means for SAP finance leaders, GRC teams, and cybersecurity stakeholders. We also explore how SAP ecosystem vendors are helping organizations strengthen financial controls, tax compliance, access governance, and SAP security as AI adoption expands.
Key topics include:
AI in SAP finance and financial close
Audit-ready AI and explainable automation
SAP GRC, access governance, and continuous controls
SAP cybersecurity and AI-related risk
Finance transformation in SAP environments
Read the blog to learn why AI governance, audit evidence, and control design now need to be part of every SAP automation strategy.

Read it here: https://lnkd.in/exNgXNSH

posts.author_name: #caia #accessrewiredreport | Nagendra Prasad KV.
posts.content:
Alternative Investments: Rewired — A View from the Ground (India & Beyond)

Attending the #CAIA India event in Mumbai, one thing became clear:
Alternative markets are not just growing—they are being fundamentally rewired.

Not by capital alone, but by data, geopolitics, and operating models.
Here’s a distilled view of where things are heading:
  
🔄 1. Public–Private Convergence is Real (but infrastructure isn’t ready)-

The boundaries are blurring—but data, reporting, and operating frameworks are still fragmented.
👉 The next wave of advantage will come from standardization and integration, not just deal-making.
  

🔍 2. LPs are asking tougher questions -

Transparency, reporting depth, and real-time visibility are no longer “nice to have.”
👉 Data is becoming the language of trust between LPs and GPs.
  

🌍 3. Alpha is increasingly geopolitical- 

Location now matters more than ever.
Capital allocation is being shaped by geopolitical shifts, not just financial models.
👉 Even sector bets—like Defence—are becoming strategic, not cyclical.
  

🧠 4. The real bottleneck: data & operations (not capital)-

As private markets scale, complexity explodes.
👉 Firms that win will be those who solve for:
*Data consistency
*Operational scalability
*AI-driven efficiency
  

🏦 5. Institutional capital is doubling down

Pension funds and long-term pools are increasing exposure to alternatives.
But this comes with a shift:
👉 Capital is patient—but expectations are not
  

📚 6. Education gap is still real
Private capital requires-

Longer horizons
Deeper understanding of illiquidity
👉 Both investors and advisors need to evolve alongside the asset class.


⚖️ 7. Transparency ≠ Public Market Replication
There’s growing demand for transparency in private markets—but:
👉 It will never mirror public markets with daily pricing and volatility
The challenge is to design:
 “fit-for-purpose transparency”
  

The CAIA The World Rewired Report 2026 reinforces this shift—highlighting how technology, data, and structural changes are reshaping the investment landscape globally. 

###AccessRewiredREPORT by scanning the QR code from the pictures below.

🇮🇳 India Sneak Peek

India is uniquely positioned in this transition
* Emerging as a global hub for data and operations in alternatives
*Increasing participation from institutional and retail channels
*Strong tailwinds from GCCs, digital infrastructure, and talent depth

👉 The real opportunity for India lies not just in capital flows, but in building the backbone of global alternative investment ecosystems
  
Great to connect with industry leaders and exchange perspectives on what’s ahead. Thanks, John L Bowman, CFA, Nick Pollard, Puneet Kumar, and Rajat Bijur, 

Thanks to Himanshu Gulliani, CAIA, and  CAIA Association - India Chapter Exco members for a great event.

The next phase of alternatives won’t be defined by who has capital—
 But by whom can operate, scale, and make sense of complexity better than others?

Alternative Investments: Rewired — A View from the Ground (India & Beyond)

Attending the #CAIA India event in Mumbai, one thing became clear:
Alternative markets are not just growing—they are being fundamentally rewired. Not by capital alone, but by data, geopolitics, and operating models. Here’s a distilled view of where things are heading:
  
🔄 1.

posts.author_name: #ai #ai #fintech #audit #compliance #enterprisesoftware #startups #automation #venturecapital #aitechsupports #fundraising #aitech #aitechnology #ceo #cto | AI Tech Supports
posts.content:
Audit and compliance are ripe for automation—and Petual is bringing agentic AI into the mix. The San Francisco-based startup has raised $20 million in funding led by Andreessen Horowitz, alongside First Round Capital, Cowboy Ventures, and Elad Gil.

Petual is building an #AI-powered platform that automates audit and compliance workflows at enterprise scale. By applying agentic AI to SOX testing and internal audits, the platform enables autonomous evidence gathering and work paper generation—tasks that have traditionally been manual, time-consuming, and resource-heavy.

The system can ingest both structured and unstructured data—including screenshots, PDFs, and Excel files—and turn them into complete audit-ready work papers within minutes, significantly improving speed and accuracy.

Already gaining traction, Petual is working with companies across the S&P 500 and NASDAQ-100 in sectors like energy, software, infrastructure, manufacturing, and financial services.

With fresh capital, the company plans to accelerate product development and expand its go-to-market efforts—positioning itself as a key player in modernising audit and compliance through AI.

Eliot Walker
Andrew Li
David Coulombe

#AI #FinTech #Audit #Compliance #EnterpriseSoftware #Startups #Automation #VentureCapital  #AITechSupports #Fundraising #AITech #AITechnology #CEO #CTO

The San Francisco-based startup has raised $20 million in funding led by Andreessen Horowitz, alongside First Round Capital, Cowboy Ventures, and Elad Gil. Petual is building an #AI-powered platform that automates audit and compliance workflows at enterprise scale.

Location: Delhi, New Delhi
Add. Qualification:

Skills required

Description

Ensure compliance with the Companies Act, 2013, SEBI regulations, and other applicable laws.
Handle compliance with Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) including LODR requirements.
Manage filings with BSE Limited and other regulatory authorities.
Oversee secretarial audits and ensure timely submission of reports.

2. Board & Committee Management

Organize Board Meetings, Committee Meetings, and Annual General Meetings (AGMs).
Prepare agendas, notices, minutes, and resolutions.
Advise the Board on governance matters and legal obligations.

3. Corporate Governance
Ensure adherence to corporate governance norms and best practices.
Maintain statutory registers and records.
Draft and update company policies in line with regulatory changes.
4. Listing Compliance
Ensure timely disclosure of financial results, shareholding patterns, and material events.
Manage insider trading compliance under SEBI (PIT) Regulations.

posts.author_name: #enterpriseai #aistrategy #digitaltransformation #datastrategy #aigovernance #cxo | Mahesh Desai
posts.content:
𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗹𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗹𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗼𝘂𝘁𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘀, 𝗮 𝗳𝗲𝘄 𝗽𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗹𝘆 𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗮𝗿.

First, modernization and AI move together. 

Legacy infrastructure, fragmented data pipelines, and siloed applications create friction that prevents AI systems from operating reliably at scale. 

Organizations that succeed typically modernize their data platforms, cloud environments, and architecture alongside AI adoption. 

Second, governance is designed from the start. 

Highly regulated industries are discovering that scaling AI safely requires automated monitoring, integrated compliance frameworks, and strong model lifecycle management. 

Third, AI systems are embedded into workflows rather than deployed as standalone tools. 

Human-in-the-loop models, intelligent automation, and AI-assisted decision systems often generate the most sustainable improvements in productivity and operational performance. 

When these elements align, the results can be significant; faster operations, reduced compliance effort, improved reliability, and measurable improvements in enterprise efficiency. 

What becomes clear very quickly is that enterprise AI is not primarily a technology challenge. 

It is a systems challenge. 

And the organizations that treat AI as an enterprise capability - not just a technology experiment - are the ones turning it into lasting competitive advantage. 

#EnterpriseAI #AIStrategy #DigitalTransformation #DataStrategy #AIGovernance #CXO 

Gajen Kandiah Dharmendra (DK) Sinha Kellie Teal-Guess Vivek Kwatra Nirmal Ranganathan Kathleen Schneider Dr. Phani Kishore Burre Mark Marino Mark Martinet 

Tanuja Randery Alison Kay Eszter Koves Lucy Swinglehurst Madhavi Reddy Anuj Sharma Nick Dosanjh Ginevra Paccagnan Ioana Roxana Ramakrishna N.
 
Simon Sharkey Eleri Gibbon Ralph Haupter Amanda Sleight Nick Hedderman Aaron Moore Naim Yazbeck Amr Kamel

posts.author_name:
Today, I’m excited to share that Petual has raised $20M to move audit from process-heavy work to judgment. 

Our raise includes a $17M round led by Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) and a $3.2M round led by… | Snir Kodesh | 102 comments
posts.content:
Today, I’m excited to share that Petual has raised $20M to move audit from process-heavy work to judgment. 

Our raise includes a $17M round led by Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) and a $3.2M round led by First Round Capital, with participation from Cowboy Ventures, Elad Gil, the founders of Lyft, Opendoor, and more. 

SOX compliance and internal audit are among the most manual, resource-intensive processes in any public company.

Auditors wade through hundreds of support files per control test. PDFs, spreadsheets, emails, screenshots, and more. Just to determine whether a control operated correctly.

Tens of millions of hours and $8B are spent on this every year.

We knew this had to change. That teams deserved to apply more of their judgment and manage risk, rather than get buried in the mechanical. That's why we built Petual.

Petual brings agentic AI to SOX testing and internal audit, autonomously gathering evidence and generating auditor-ready work papers in minutes instead of hours.

Our customers -- S&P 500 and NASDAQ 100 companies spanning energy, infrastructure, SaaS, manufacturing, financial services, and more -- can see 68–80% efficiency gains on SOX workflows.

Huge thanks to our investors, early customers like Navan and Eos Energy Enterprises, Inc., and the many other customers who’ve been part of this journey; and of course, our incredible team.

If you're in audit or compliance and want to see Petual in action, let’s talk.
 | 102 comments on LinkedIn

posts.author_name: #informationgovernance #ai | Rob Gerbrandt CD, PMP, IGP, FIIM
posts.content:
Records Management and Information Governance professionals are feeling a very real sense of FOMO right now. AI is being marketed as the answer to almost every content problem, from classification and search to retention, redaction, and compliance, and that creates pressure on professionals who have spent years building disciplined, policy-driven systems. The tension is understandable: AI can genuinely improve records work, but the hype often moves faster than governance, strategy, and readiness.
For records and information governance teams, the anxiety is not just about missing a trend. It is about watching other business functions adopt AI tools that appear to do in seconds what traditional systems have required people, rules, and audits to accomplish carefully. Articles and industry commentary consistently point to AI use cases such as automated classification, intelligent search, sensitive-data detection, and lifecycle management as major benefits for records work.
That creates an uncomfortable comparison. A records professional may ask why they still need to chase users for metadata, train staff on file plans, or manually review retention triggers when an AI product promises to do it all. The answer is that AI can help, but it does not eliminate the need for governance, policy, or accountability. In fact, AI often exposes weak governance more clearly rather than fixing it.
This is where I've been focusing these past few months. We're building what we term as Human Augmented AI Powered IG. It's not, by any means a super tool that will replace you. Instead, we want to help you and your colleagues improve how content is classified according to your rules, your retention schedule and your risk tolerances. 
Watch this space for more details! If you're interested in learning more or being part of our early adopter community, DM me!

#InformationGovernance
#AI

posts.author_name: #aiatfi #bankingai #bcg #bcgx #generativeai | Nipun Kalra
posts.content:
Not a prototype. Not a pilot. AI in production, at scale, inside a bank.

This is the hardest thing right now to do in financial services  - and this team did it. Aparna Kapoor and Vincent Paca 
 
Many AI initiatives in FIs die between demo and deployment. The model works. The business case works. Then reality hits: legacy infrastructure, risk frameworks, change governance, audit trails. The initiative drifts. The value slows down.

What this team cracked is the layer most AI programs never invest in: delivery architecture.
Harness engineering gave them release velocity with rollback confidence - the combination banks actually need to move fast without breaking things that regulators, risk teams, and operations cannot afford to have broken.

> The winning teams aren’t just better at AI. They’re also better at engineering the operating system around AI.
> Governance. Pipeline discipline. Auditability baked in - not bolted on.

Saurabh Tripathi Dr. Stiene Riemer Sylvain Duranton Nicolas de Bellefonds  

#AIatFI #BankingAI #BCG #BCGX #GenerativeAI
posts.summary: Not a prototype. Not a pilot. AI in production, at scale, inside a bank.

posts.author_name: Shadow AI, Real Risk
posts.content:
Your employees are already using Artificial Intelligence (AI). The question is whether you know which tools, which data, and which decisions depend on them.

Most organizations do not have an AI strategy problem first. They have a visibility and governance problem. Public Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) AI tools are proliferating faster than any policy can chase them, and sensitive data is entering prompts with little to no oversight. This is Shadow AI, and the risk is real.

There is a useful lesson in how spreadsheet macros evolved inside many enterprises. They often started as small productivity enhancements, but over time they became embedded in important financial and operational processes without proper documentation, resilience planning, or clear accountability. Mini "critical applications". When the original creator moved on, the organization was left with a fragile dependency that few people fully understood. Artificial Intelligence agents and AI-enabled workflows can follow a similar pattern, only faster and with broader consequences.

Governing AI means inventorying not just the application name but the underlying foundation model, the AI agents running on top of it, and every AI-enabled workflow woven into operations. Most organizations have not started that inventory.

Frameworks like the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) AI Risk Management Framework (RMF) and ISO/IEC 42001 provide the right foundation. The real challenge is translating them into something people actually operate day to day. That is where a practical operating model matters. The GOES model: Governance, Oversight, Execution, and Support, gives security and risk leaders a structure that works at the speed enterprises actually move.

The organizations that get this right will not be the ones with the most sophisticated AI strategy. They will be the ones that built governance before the dependency became invisible.
Read the full article at TheCISOBlog.com

https://lnkd.in/gZG_RAP3

Anna Pieczatkowska 🍸 one of your recent posts inspired me to write this one! “AI adoption outran governance”

#CyberRisk #CISO #AIGovernance #ShadowAI #CyberGovernance #RiskManagement #ArtificialIntelligence #NISTaiRMF
posts.summary: Your employees are already using Artificial Intelligence (AI). The question is whether you know which tools, which data, and which decisions depend on them. Most organizations do not have an AI strategy problem first.
